coredata.dc:title: Biogenic raw materials from food waste and by-products for smart packaging applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Current Opinion in Green and Sustainable Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Matthes, Julia
Schmid, Markus
coredata.dc:description: Innovative smart packaging concepts are seen as crucial in addressing the challenges of sustainable food production. In this brief review paper, recent advancements in smart packaging are explored, with a focus on biogenic raw materials derived from agro-food by-products and waste. The primary goal is to develop environmentally sustainable systems that enhance food quality, extend shelf life, and thereby reduce economic and environmental losses. This paper briefly reviews recent findings on antioxidative and antimicrobial packaging concepts that delay food deterioration. One focus is on encapsulating natural substances and carbon dots in active films to boost their performance. The paper also discusses pH-sensitive anthocyanin-based indicators for assessing food freshness and analyzes strategies to stabilize these indicators, including encapsulation, enzyme inactivation, and multilayer film design. This review aims to provide guidance for future research and application in the field of sustainable smart packaging, offering innovative solutions for food preservation and food waste reduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smart packaging
Anthocyanin
Food freshness indicator
Antioxidative
Antimicrobial
Waste

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Innovations in active packaging developed from food waste and by-products
Innovations in intelligent packaging developed from food waste and by-products
Safety considerations for the utilization of biogenic raw materials from food waste and by-products in smart packaging
Challenges preceding a potential market launch of smart packaging concepts containing biogenic raw materials from food waste and by-products

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Ms. Fehime Nur Aday for her excellent assistance while revising the manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview over smart packaging (active and intelligent) concepts for food packaging (own illustration based on [6,7,41–44]).
Schematic image of an active packaging concept adapted from [45].

Schematic image of an intelligent freshness indicator (a) adapted from [45]. Color change of an intelligent sodium alginate/polyvinyl alcohol film containing purple garlic peel extract (10 wt.%) and TiO2 nanoparticles (1wt.%) during beef storage in sterile PET boxes at 4 °C ± 1 °C (b) adapted from [46]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Current studies (2022, 2023) which focus on antioxidative and antimicrobial packaging concepts using food waste and by-products as active ingredient and their application to perishable or high lipid containing food.

row.entry: Results of the storage test (end of storage, unless otherwise stated)

LDPE/4% LE
PLA/PEG/4% LE
PLA/6% LE, lemon by-product extract (LE)

LDPE/4% LE most effective active film; slight delay of lipid oxidation in almonds compared to LDPE
PLA/6% LE (control PLA): reduction of lipid oxidation between day 4–6, day 8–11 higher lipid oxidation
Reduction of bacterial growth
Pectin, PEG, grapefruit peel extract, maltodextrin encapsulated lemon peel extract
Tomatoes (artificially contaminated with E. coli) 4 °C, 6 days
Reduction of initial E. coli counts (control unwrapped and pectin films without active substance)
Gelatin, PVA, grapefruit seed extract, carbon dots

Reduction in photo-oxidation in ground pork
Reduction of Listeria monocytogenes growth in minced pork (control LDPE)
PLA substrate, konjac glucomannan, wheat gluten, glycerol, spent coffee waste extracts
Reduction of lipid peroxidation (controls: film without active substance, unwrapped sample)
Blue crab shells, Sardinella aurita by-products

Reduction of bacterial growth
Reduction of lipid peroxidation (control unpacked sample)
1% beetroot peel extract, gelatin, alginate, glycerol

Reduction of protein and lipid oxidation
Reduction of bacterial growth (control: film without active substance)
Guar gum, CMC, halloysite, glycerol nanotubes, lichi shell extract, PE outer layer
Reduction of lipid oxidation (control: film without active substance)
Starch, CMC, gelatin, tea ground waste, cellulose, glycerol

Reduction of bacterial growth at early stages of storage (day 3-6)
Total amount of biogenic amines decreased
No significant differences in lipid oxidation (control: LDPE)
Furcellaran, soybean bran protein hydrolysate, glycerol; soybean husk extract (in bilayer films)

25 °C no significant differences in lipid oxidation; exception TBARS in bilayer films with protein hydrolysate and husk extract significant higher
4 °C significant increase in lipid oxidation (control: LDPE)

No significant differences in microbial growth
No significant differences in lipid oxidation (control: LDPE)
Chitosan, guar gum, walnut green husk extract

Reduction of browning index (only films containing 2 and 4% active extract)
Reduction of microbial growth (control: PE and chitosan/guar gum film without active extract)
Extracts from red chilto peel, pectin enriched extract

Reduction of protein oxidation
Reduction of lipid oxidation (control: unwrapped sample)
2 layers cellulose laminated by bio-degradable adhesive containing 8% carob seed macerates

No significant differences in pH
Reduction of TVB-N
Reduction of lipid oxidation (control: cellulose multilayer without active substance)
Chitosan or PVA, acorn shell extract (1, 5, 10%)

Chitosan films: reduction of lipid oxidation in all active films
PVA films: slight reduction of lipid oxidation (1, 5% active extract), significant increase in lipid oxidation at 10% active extract
(control: chitosan or PVA films without active substance)
PVA, starch, glycerol, watermelon rind extract, film wrapped on PET container

Slight reduction of initial microbial counts
Controls: increase in microbial counts (control: thermoplastic starch film wrapped on PET container)
Chitosan, glycerol, grape seed extract-silver nanoparticles or PVP silver nanoparticles
Reduction of yeast and mold growth (controls: chitosan coating, untreated)
Chitosan, PEG, date palm fruit extract, glycerol, glyoxal
Reduction of bacterial growth (controls: chitosan/PEG film without active substance, unwrapped)

head.ce:title: Biogenic raw materials from food waste and by-products for smart packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sustainable Packaging Institute SPI, Faculty of Life Sciences, Albstadt-Sigmaringen University, Anton-Günther-Straße 51, 72488 Sigmaringen, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sustainable Packaging Institute SPI, Faculty of Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Sciences, Albstadt-Sigmaringen University, Anton-Gunther- Strasse 51, 72488 Sigmaringen, Germany

head.ce:miscellaneous: This review comes from a themed issue on Bioactive compounds from agri-food byproducts and wastes
Edited by Khaoula Khwaldia and Abdennacer Boulila

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Innovative smart packaging concepts are seen as crucial in addressing the challenges of sustainable food production. In this brief review paper, recent advancements in smart packaging are explored, with a focus on biogenic raw materials derived from agro-food by-products and waste. The primary goal is to develop environmentally sustainable systems that enhance food quality, extend shelf life, and thereby reduce economic and environmental losses. This paper briefly reviews recent findings on antioxidative and antimicrobial packaging concepts that delay food deterioration. One focus is on encapsulating natural substances and carbon dots in active films to boost their performance. The paper also discusses pH-sensitive anthocyanin-based indicators for assessing food freshness and analyzes strategies to stabilize these indicators, including encapsulation, enzyme inactivation, and multilayer film design. This review aims to provide guidance for future research and application in the field of sustainable smart packaging, offering innovative solutions for food preservation and food waste reduction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:sections.ce:para: For complete overview of the section, please refer the article collection - Bioactive compounds from agri-food byproducts and wastes

ce:section.ce:para: Food waste and loss have a significant influence on the environment. In the European Union food waste and loss is associated to 16% (264 Mt CO2eq.) of the greenhouse gas emissions within the European food system [1]. A study in the United States demonstrated that 16–18% of the environmental impacts of its food system could be attributed to food loss and waste. Particularly loss and waste reductions in the segments foodservice, food processing and household consumption should be realized [2]. Food waste in German households is estimated to be 75 kg/capita (6.3 million tonnes/year) [3]. A survey with 1000 persons in Germany revealed that 58% of all respondents discarded food because of the fast deterioration of the product (perishable food), 37% because of insecurities concerning the freshness and 35% because the best before date had expired [4].

Smart packaging combines active and intelligent packaging concepts [5]. Active packaging attempts to preserve the status quo of the food and to delay its deterioration, while intelligent packaging either monitors (sensors) or visually communicates the freshness of the food (indicators) or provides information (data carrier) [6,7]. Figure 1 shows an overview of smart packaging concepts.

Oxidation, enzymatic, and microbial processes lead to the deterioration of perishable food. As a result, spoilage indicating substances like organic acids or volatile nitrogenous compounds can be formed within the food and influence its pH [8]. While active antioxidative and antimicrobial packaging aims to delay oxidative and microbial deterioration processes [9], food freshness indicators (FFIs) are used to visually determine the freshness of a packaged good by a pH-sensitive color system integrated into the packaging [10]. Conventional FFIs often use chromogenic dyes like phenol red, bromophenol red, bromophenol blue, bromocresol violet, bromocresol green, methyl red, or polyaniline [11]. Safety issues due to the migration of the dye into food have to be considered, as some of these dyes are associated with adverse effects on human health like toxicity and carcinogenicity [12].

The food industry, especially the fruit and vegetable processing sector produces abundant amounts of waste, which is usually disposed of by animal feeding, landfill, composting or thermal treatment [13]. These wastes often contain valuable bioactive compounds like polyphenols, carotenoids, phytosterols, or glucosinolates. Polyphenols for example show an antioxidative and often an antimicrobial activity and are of major interest for the development of active packaging (Figure 2 ) [14], while anthocyanin can be used to construct FFIs due to their pH-dependent coloring [15].

Food waste reduction and valorization strategies are instruments to decrease the environmental burden of the food system. The effectiveness of valorization techniques depend on various factors such as the kind of food waste, refining and conversation processes or the end use of the newly developed products. Valorization of food waste and by-products promotes the development of value added products (for example polyhydroxyalkanoates, phenolics) and contributes towards a more circular economy [16]. Smart packaging concepts containing bioactive raw materials have the potential to address both food waste reduction and valorization. This review focuses on recent advancements in the development and improvement of intelligent anthocyanin based FFIs and active antimicrobial and antioxidative packaging. Of particular interest is the valorization of low-cost biogenic raw materials from food waste and by-products of the agro-food industry. As natural based extracts and compounds are used in smart packaging concepts, the safety of said innovative substances within the food chain has to be carefully evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Innovations in active packaging developed from food waste and by-products
ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic substances are of major interest in the development of active antioxidative and antimicrobial packaging. Table 1 summarizes a selection of current studies, which focus on packaging concepts using food waste and by-products as active ingredient and their application to perishable or high lipid containing food. In order to verify their effectivity, storage tests are an important parameter in the development of packaging concepts. This could be demonstrated in a study using lemon by-product extract as active substance in LDPE (4% active extract) or PLA (4, 6% active extract) films. Whereas only low amounts of phenolic substances were released from LDPE/4%lemon extract into a simulant representing highly fat containing food, significantly increased concentrations were detected for PLA/lemon extract films. Consequently, no antioxidative activity was detected in LDPE/4%lemon extract films after conducting DPPH radical assay, in which migration of antioxidants into a test solution is prerequisite, while PLA films containing 4 and 6% lemon extract showed an antioxidative effect. However, a subsequent storage trial with roasted almonds revealed that LDPE/4%lemon extract was the most effective packaging material for this product. While a majority of the results of this active packaging is comparable to those of LDPE, during the first 15d roasted almonds stored in LDPE/4%lemon extract showed significantly lower concentrations of p-anisidine, an indicator of lipid oxidation [17].

The use of encapsulation techniques or the utilization of nanoparticles can enhance the functionality of active antioxidative and antimicrobial films. For instance, the encapsulation of lemon peel extract more than doubled the phenolic concentration in comparison to the neat extract. Consequently, also an increased antimicrobial and antioxidative capacity was observed. Films containing pectin and 0.04% (w/v) grapefruit peel/maltodextrin-encapsulated lemon extract were even able to reduce the total counts of E. coli in inoculated halved tomatoes after 6d (4 °C) of storage by approximately 1.6log while the concentration in samples wrapped in pectin films or unwrapped tomatoes increased during storage [18].

Min et al. [19] demonstrated that carbon dot nanoparticles (CD) produced from spent coffee ground have a high antioxidative capacity. A gelatin/PVA film with 0.08 wt.% (of film forming solution, FFS) CD showed significant higher antioxidative activity than using grape seed extract (0.08 wt.% FFS) as active substance, while the extract was more effective in inhibiting microbial growth. Best results were obtained by combining both active substances (0.08 wt.% FFS). The growth of Listeria monocytogenes could be significantly delayed after storing minced meat for 48h (20 °C) with the active film compared to LDPE control samples, also the photo-oxidation of ground pork was significantly decreased during the same storage conditions but using additional UV-irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Innovations in intelligent packaging developed from food waste and by-products
ce:section.ce:para: Recent research in FFI using biogenic colorants focuses mainly on anthocyanin. Anthocyanin can be detected in orange-, red-, purple-, or blue-colored parts of various fruit and vegetables. Subsequently, waste from the food processing industry represent a promising source of raw materials for the exploitation of these natural colorants [20]. The advantage of using anthocyanin in freshness indicating systems is their pH-dependent change in color due to structural transformations [21]. Moreover, copigmentation or the presence of metal ions may also influence its color. The addition of a betalain rich powder from beetroot improved the sensitivity of a cassava starch pH-indicator film with red cabbage anthocyanin during the storage of hake fillets [22], while the presence of Al3+ ions in film forming solutions based on carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)/gelatin/montmorillonite and red cabbage anthocyanin led to a permanent change of color [23].

FFIs containing anthocyanin as pH-indicating colorant are used to visualize the freshness of perishable food, like fish, seafood, or meat (Figure 3 ). TVB-N (total volatile basic nitrogen) is a parameter, which includes several volatile nitrogenous substances that are formed during the storage of protein-rich food and is commonly used to evaluate fish and seafood freshness [24]. This is in accordance with the results of a study monitoring the freshness of shrimp using a gelatin/chitosan/nano-ZnO film with black peanut skin anthocyanin. TVB-N and pH increased during the storage. The change of the pH was indicated by a shift of color from red (0d, fresh) to light red (2d) and light green (4d, deteriorated) and which was strongly correlated to the amount of TVB-N. The deterioration of the stored shrimp after 4d was also confirmed by microbiological analysis demonstrating the suitability of the FFI system [25].

The main challenge in the utilization of anthocyanin in FFIs is their instability in various environments. They are prone to degradation, which can be induced by pH, temperature, light, oxygen, solvents, enzymes, or several reactive substances [20,26]. A suitable method for stabilizing anthocyanin in FFIs could be encapsulation techniques. Anthocyanin from grape skin could be successfully encapsulated into ZIF-8 a metal-organic framework consisting of Zn2+ and 2-methylimidazole. Bacterial nanocellulose/PVA films containing ZIF-8 encapsulated anthocyanin successfully indicated the deterioration of cold stored chicken [27]. In addition, different cooking techniques using purple sweet potatoes showed a positive effect on the stability of anthocyanin. After a gelating step, which is part of the film preparation the total anthocyanin content, total phenol content and antioxidative activity of raw purple sweet potato powder decreased considerably while being only slightly reduced in the cooked variants. The authors postulated that this effect could be due to the inactivation of enzymes and the embedding of the anthocyanin in a gelling network based on starch formed during the cooking process of the purple sweet potatoes [28]. Especially, dye leaching is a problem in anthocyanin containing indicator films. It could be observed that soybean polysaccharide/graphene oxide films containing 5wt.% anthocyanin rich grape skin extract released >90% of the extract into aqueous food simulants (water, 10% ethanol) in only 4–6h [29]. A possibility to increase the stability of anthocyanin in FFIs can be the formation of multilayer films. Dye leaching of gelatin/chitosan/0.5% (w/v) red cabbage extract - CMC multilayer films prepared with the help of a casting technique was estimated using two food simulants (water, 95% ethanol). An improved stability of the dye could be observed in multilayer films compared to monolayer films of chitosan +0.5% (w/v) red cabbage extract. Whereas anthocyanins were identified in monolayer films by FTIR in both food simulants after an incubation time of 10d, dye leaching was not observed in multilayer films evaluated with 95% ethanol [30].

A smart packaging concept was developed using a CMC/gelatin/montmorillonite film with red cabbage anthocyanin. The addition of pistacia leaf extract as active agent lead to an antioxidative and antimicrobial effect of the films. During a storage test with shrimp at ambient temperature, different CMC/gelatin/montmorillonite films formulations were evaluated. The films had no direct contact to the packaged shrimps. While the color of films, which only contained anthocyanin, shifted from blue to blue-greenish after 3d indicating an increase of the pH, films with pistacia leaf extract and anthocyanin could only detect pH changes after 7d of storage. The deterioration of shrimp was delayed in all films containing the active antioxidative and antimicrobial agent pistacia leaf extract based on a visual examination of the shrimp [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Safety considerations for the utilization of biogenic raw materials from food waste and by-products in smart packaging
ce:section.ce:para: Valuable biogenic substances gained from waste or by-products of the agro-food processing industry have to be safe regarding the human organism before they are included in smart packaging. Data concerning the toxicity of natural substances are often scarce. No adverse effects are associated with anthocyanin based on a regular diet. Although it was suggested that anthocyanin might be harmful for the human organism when consumed at very high concentrations, a complete overview of toxicological studies is still missing [31].

When new sources of raw materials for commercial purposes are developed, an evaluation concerning the security of the respective substance/extract is important. In order to assess the suitability of by-products and waste from the olive oil production, Khwaldia et al. [32] evaluated the toxicity of important phenolic substances, possible residues and contaminants like pesticides, mycotoxins, or heavy metals. However, before the natural substance/extract can be commercially used in packaging concepts, the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has to approve of the respective substance. To assess its safety, migration and toxicological properties of the biogenic substance/extract as well as possible degradation and reaction products have to be evaluated [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges preceding a potential market launch of smart packaging concepts containing biogenic raw materials from food waste and by-products
ce:section.ce:para: To our knowledge, there are currently no smart packaging concepts on the European market, which contain natural active or intelligent substances from biogenic origin. In order to achieve market readiness those packaging concepts have to overcome several techno-economic challenges and fulfill the requirements of an industrial scale up.

The techniques used for the production of the active or intelligent layer of the smart packaging concepts have to be scalable. The most abundant technique used in the studies discussed in Sections Innovations in active packaging developed from food waste and by-products and Innovations in intelligent packaging developed from food waste and by-products is solvent casting but also coating, electrospinning and direct melting followed by hot compression are used. The first three methods are good alternatives when using temperature sensitive substances in the film formulation. Extrusion, the most common technique in the packaging industry, usually requires high temperatures to melt the polymers [34]. Anthocyanins from butterfly pea blue petals for example show thermal stability between 60 and 70 °C but already decrease between 80 and 100 °C [35] which could potentially lead to an decrease of the biogenic substance when using extrusion techniques.

In addition, other technoeconomical aspects such as the compatibility of different components of a smart packaging concept, the technical reliability of the packaging considering the packaged good, processing steps and storage as well as the sustainability of said packaging concept have to be taken into account [36,37]. For instance, a comparison between a smart packaging concept using encapsulated oregano essential oil (1% w/w) as active and a chemical CO2 sensor (ZnO based) as intelligent feature with the corresponding conventional packaging showed an increased environmental burden of the smart packaging in the production step. However, as the organic waste End of Life has a severe impact on the environmental performance of a food packaging concept an estimated reduction of food waste (5, 10, 20% smart vs. 30% conventional) significantly improved the performance of the smart packaging concept [38].

Besides, the smart packaging concept has to be in accordance with the current legislation (e.g. Commission Regulation (EC) No 450/2009), regulatory standards and certifications [36,37,39]. Consumer acceptance, the acceptance of industry and costs (development costs, price of the smart packaging concept) are crucial drivers in the market. The first two aspects depend highly on the trust of the stakeholder in the technology of the smart packaging concept, which requires education, transfer of knowledge and communication [36,37]. This was confirmed by a qualitative study using a smart label to monitor meat freshness. While 83% of the three focus groups (n = 18) had a positive impression towards the smart label, there were still concerns concerning the accuracy of the label (33.3%, n = 6) or its effectiveness while the product is frozen (16.7%, n = 3). 38.9% of the respondents (n = 7) trusted the label after the expiration date only in combination with their own sensory impression or when the package was still unopened [40]. Finally, to conclude this section with the aspect costs, a recent study evaluating the willingness of consumers (1249 individuals from 16 different European countries, born between 1980 and 2000) to invest more money for active or intelligent packaging observed, that 72.9% of the respondents were either unwilling to pay more money or less than 10% more for active packaging respectively 66.4% for intelligent packaging [37].

Storage trials of perishable food and those susceptible to oxidation could demonstrate the potential of antioxidative and antimicrobial active packaging based on different food waste and by-products. Encapsulation techniques or the use of carbon dots in active antioxidative and antimicrobial films can enhance their functionality.

Additionally, anthocyanin freshness indicators are sensitive and safe for use in the food industry. The use of anthocyanin from biogenic sources is still limited due to its structural instability. However, successful approaches have been developed to increase its stability, such as encapsulation, enzyme inactivation, and the development of protective multilayer systems.

A promising concept is the use of anthocyanin as a pH-indicator in smart packaging, combined with an antimicrobial and antioxidative active film. To reduce food waste throughout the supply chain, it is valuable to maintain food quality, extend shelf life and visually indicate freshness of perishable food. However, developing stable and low-cost smart packaging concepts that are accepted by the food industry requires further research.

In addition to discussing the key findings and potential of biogenic raw materials in smart packaging, it is important to highlight their broader implications for society and the environment. These innovative packaging solutions represent a paradigm shift towards more sustainable food handling practices. By utilizing bio-based materials, this approach aligns with circular bio-economy principles turning waste into valuable resources. The societal impact of these advancements extends beyond food preservation and includes significant reductions in food waste and associated environmental consequences. Furthermore, these advancements hold promise for enhancing global food security by preserving the quality of perishables. As research progresses, the adoption of smart packaging technologies could usher in a new era of food sustainability, in line with the increasing consumer demand for environmentally responsible practices. Continued innovation and research in this field are essential to overcome current limitations and fully realize the potential of these technologies in everyday use.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
Julia Matthes: Conceptualization; Writing–original draft; Writing–review and editing. Markus Schmid: Conceptualization; Supervision; Writing–review and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Ms. Fehime Nur Aday for her excellent assistance while revising the manuscript.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biogenic amine detection systems for intelligent packaging concepts: meat and meat products
Food Rev Int

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Miller, K.; Reichert, C.L.; Schmid, M. Biogenic Amine Detection Systems for Intelligent Packaging Concepts: Meat and Meat Products. Food Reviews International, 2023, 39, 2543-2567.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on the challenges and choices for food waste valorization: environmental and economic impacts
ACS environmental Au

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Roy, P.; Mohanty, A.K.; Dick, P.; Misra, M. A Review on the Challenges and Choices for Food Waste Valorization: Environmental and Economic Impacts. ACS environmental Au, 2023, 3, 58-75.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: LDPE and PLA active food packaging incorporated with lemon by-products extract: preparation, characterization and effectiveness to delay lipid oxidation in almonds and beef meat
Foods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Andrade, M.A.; Barbosa, C.H.; Marino-Cortegoso, S.; Barbosa-Pereira, L.; Sendon, R.; Buonocore, G.G.; Stanzione, M.; Coelho, A.; Correia, C.B.; Saraiva, M.; Quiros, A.R.-B. de; Vilarinho, F.; Khwaldia, K.; Silva, A.S.; Ramos, F. LDPE and PLA Active Food Packaging Incorporated with Lemon by-Products Extract: Preparation, Characterization and Effectiveness to Delay Lipid Oxidation in Almonds and Beef Meat. Foods (Basel, Switzerland), 2023, 12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel active edible food packaging films based entirely on citrus peel wastes
Food Hydrocolloids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Khalil, R.K.; Sharaby, M.R.; Abdelrahim, D.S. Novel active edible food packaging films based entirely on citrus peel wastes. Food Hydrocolloids, 2023, 134, 107961.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: The authors used substances derived from lemon peel wastes, especially from grapefruit peels like pectin and different extracts to develop an antioxidative and antimicrobial film, which was able to reduce the amount of E.coli in halved tomatoes. This study uses almost exclusively low value wastes especially from grapefruit peels to improve the microbial stability of perishable, pre-cut vegetables. This could be a chance for low-income countries to produce cheap packaging concepts and reduce wastes at the same time.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol)-based functional films integrated with spent coffee ground-derived carbon dots and grapefruit seed extract for active packaging application
Int J Biol Macromol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Min, S.; Ezati, P.; Yoon, K.S.; Rhim, J.-W. Gelatin/poly(vinyl alcohol)-based functional films integrated with spent coffee ground-derived carbon dots and grapefruit seed extract for active packaging application. International Journal of Biological Macromolecules, 2023, 231, 123493.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: The authors used an active extract and carbon dot nanoparticles both made of wastes and by-products of the food industry. Use of carbon dot nanoparticles significantly increased the antioxidative capacity of the active films.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Natural anthocyanins: sources, extraction, characterization, and suitability for smart packaging
Food Packag Shelf Life

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Oladzadabbasabadi, N.; Mohammadi Nafchi, A.; Ghasemlou, M.; Ariffin, F.; Singh, Z.; Al-Hassan, A. Natural anthocyanins: Sources, extraction, characterization, and suitability for smart packaging. Food Packaging and Shelf Life, 2022, 33, 100872.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biodegradable intelligent film for food preservation and real-time visual detection of food freshness
Food Hydrocolloids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, Y.; Du, J.; Zhou, H.; Zhou, S.; Lv, Y.; Cheng, Y.; Tao, Y.; Lu, J.; Wang, H. Biodegradable intelligent film for food preservation and real-time visual detection of food freshness. Food Hydrocolloids, 2022, 129, 107665.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cassava starch films with anthocyanins and betalains from agroindustrial by-products: their use for intelligent label development
Foods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Otalora Gonzalez, C.M.; Schelegueda, L.I.; Pizones Ruiz-Henestrosa, V.M.; Campos, C.A.; Basanta, M.F.; Gerschenson, L.N. Cassava Starch Films with Anthocyanins and Betalains from Agroindustrial by-Products: Their Use for Intelligent Label Development. Foods (Basel, Switzerland), 2022, 11.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cross-linked CMC/Gelatin bio-nanocomposite films with organoclay, red cabbage anthocyanins and pistacia leaves extract as active intelligent food packaging: colorimetric pH indication, antimicrobial/antioxidant properties, and shrimp spoilage tests
Int J Biol Macromol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sadi, A.; Ferfera-Harrar, H. Cross-linked CMC/Gelatin bio-nanocomposite films with organoclay, red cabbage anthocyanins and pistacia leaves extract as active intelligent food packaging: colorimetric pH indication, antimicrobial/antioxidant properties, and shrimp spoilage tests. International Journal of Biological Macromolecules, 2023, 242, 124964.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comprehensive review on freshness of fish and assessment: analytical methods and recent innovations
Food Res Int

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Prabhakar, P.K.; Vatsa, S.; Srivastav, P.P.; Pathak, S.S. A comprehensive review on freshness of fish and assessment: Analytical methods and recent innovations. Food Research International, 2020, 133, 109157.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric indicator based on chitosan/gelatin with nano-ZnO and black peanut seed coat anthocyanins for application in intelligent packaging
Food Res Int

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lu, M.; Zhou, Q.; Yu, H.; Chen, X.; Yuan, G. Colorimetric indicator based on chitosan/gelatin with nano-ZnO and black peanut seed coat anthocyanins for application in intelligent packaging. Food Research International, 2022, 160, 111664.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: A freshness indicator based on chitosan, gelatin, nano-ZnO and anthocyanin was evaluated by a storage test using shrimp. The authors analyzed several parameters, which indicate deterioration in shrimp like an increasing TVB-N, the total viable count of microorganisms and the amount of thiobarbituric acid. All parameters showed a good correlation to the color change in the freshness indicator demonstrating its suitability.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An eco-friendly film of pH-responsive indicators for smart packaging
J Food Eng

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cheng, M.; Yan, X.; Cui, Y.; Han, M.; Wang, X.; Wang, J.; Zhang, R. An eco-friendly film of pH-responsive indicators for smart packaging. Journal of Food Engineering, 2022, 321, 110943.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Food freshness monitoring using poly(vinyl alcohol) and anthocyanins doped zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 multilayer films with bacterial nanocellulose beneath as support
Carbohydr Polym

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Oktay, C.; Kahyaoglu, L.N.; Moradi, M. Food freshness monitoring using poly(vinyl alcohol) and anthocyanins doped zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 multilayer films with bacterial nanocellulose beneath as support. Carbohydrate Polymers, 2023, 319, 121184.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: Zeolitic imidazole framework-8 nanocrystals were used for the encapsulation of anthocyanin in order to increase their stability in a pH-sensitive multilayer film. The indicator was able to visualize the freshness of chicken by demonstrating a close relationship between changes in film color and an increase in TVB-N and total viable count of microorganisms.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new method to prepare color-changeable smart packaging films based on the cooked purple sweet potato
Food Hydrocolloids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, C.; Sun, J.; Yun, D.; Wang, Z.; Tang, C.; Liu, J. A new method to prepare color-changeable smart packaging films based on the cooked purple sweet potato. Food Hydrocolloids, 2023, 137, 108397.

ce:note.ce:simple-para: An intelligent film with a pH-sensitive coloring was developed by an easy and comparably low cost approach. The use of anthocyanin rich purple sweet potato powder gained from steamed or boiled potatoes in intelligent freshness indicator films showed a beneficial effect on anthocyanin stability, color sensitivity, mechanical, thermal and barrier properties compared to powders of raw or roasted potatoes.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanocomposites of soluble soybean polysaccharides with grape skin anthocyanins and graphene oxide as an efficient halochromic smart packaging
Sustainable Materials and Technologies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kafashan, A.; Joze-Majidi, H.; Kazemi-Pasarvi, S.; Babaei, A.; Jafari, S.M. Nanocomposites of soluble soybean polysaccharides with grape skin anthocyanins and graphene oxide as an efficient halochromic smart packaging. Sustainable Materials and Technologies, 2023, 38, e00755.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication and characterization of intelligent multi-layered biopolymer film incorporated with pH-sensitive red cabbage extract to indicate fish freshness
Polymers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zam, M.; Niyumsut, I.; Osako, K.; Rawdkuen, S. Fabrication and Characterization of Intelligent Multi-Layered Biopolymer Film Incorporated with pH-Sensitive Red Cabbage Extract to Indicate Fish Freshness. Polymers, 2022, 14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anthocyanins, vibrant color pigments, and their role in skin cancer prevention
Biomedicines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Diaconeasa, Z.; Știrbu, I.; Xiao, J.; Leopold, N.; Ayvaz, Z.; Danciu, C.; Ayvaz, H.; Stǎnilǎ, A.; Nistor, M.; Socaciu, C. Anthocyanins, Vibrant Color Pigments, and Their Role in Skin Cancer Prevention. Biomedicines, 2020, 8.

Compr Rev Food Sci Food Saf

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Khwaldia, K.; Attour, N.; Matthes, J.; Beck, L.; Schmid, M. Olive byproducts and their bioactive compounds as a valuable source for food packaging applications. Comprehensive reviews in food science and food safety, 2022, 21, 1218-1253.
